query: Terrion Arnold Detroit Lions Commissioner's Exempt List released waived NFL transaction status July 2026
answer:
Research complete. Here's the key finding on the research question:

**Terrion Arnold was NOT placed on the Commissioner's Exempt List.** Instead, the Detroit Lions **released (waived) him outright on June 29, 2026** — hours after he posted a $1 million bond — and he **cleared waivers unclaimed on Monday, July 6, 2026**, making him an unrestricted free agent.

Key points:
- **Transaction mechanism**: Straight release → waivers → cleared unclaimed → free agent. No Commissioner's Exempt List designation appears in any source reviewed.
- **Legal context**: The 23-year-old 2024 first-round pick (Alabama) faces eight felony charges (armed robbery/kidnapping) from a February 2026 incident; case is before Judge Christopher C. Sabella (FL 13th Circuit).
- **Current status (as of July 6 reporting)**: Unsigned free agent; attorney Harvey Steinberg reported at least three NFL teams had contacted him, but no signing had been reported.

Sourced across NFL.com, ESPN, Detroit News, Pro Football Rumors, NBC Sports/PFT, Pride of Detroit, and On3. Full structured output with sources and data points has been submitted.

key_facts: Terrion Arnold was released/waived by the Detroit Lions on June 29, 2026, hours after posting a $1 million bond in his Florida criminal case.
Arnold cleared waivers UNCLAIMED on Monday, July 6, 2026, becoming an unrestricted free agent (reported by NFL Network's Mike Garafolo, ESPN, Detroit News, Pro Football Rumors, NBC Sports/PFT).
Arnold was NOT placed on the Commissioner's Exempt List; the Lions chose to release him outright rather than use that designation. No source indicates Exempt List placement.
Arnold, age 23, was the Detroit Lions' 2024 first-round draft pick out of Alabama.

He faces eight felony charges from a February 2026 incident: three counts of armed robbery, one conspiracy to commit armed robbery, three counts of kidnapping, and one conspiracy to commit kidnapping.
His attorney Harvey Steinberg reported at least three NFL teams had contacted Arnold, but no new team had signed him as of the reporting cutoff.
The criminal case is before Judge Christopher C. Sabella of Florida's 13th Circuit Court, with a pending decision on GPS monitoring/house arrest pending trial.
